description: Shared conformance cases for the Code evaluation core. Each case's `expr` is a Code AST (the JSON shape authored in instance.schema.json); both the C# and TypeScript interpreters must evaluate it to `expect`. Scalar results only, so the two implementations can be compared directly.

cases.name: int literal
ambient provide resolves in a callee (flows down)
ambient shadow — nearest provider wins
ambient pops on block exit — caller unaffected by a callee's provide
context: a staged write reads back from the overlay (base untouched)
context: discard drops staged writes — base unchanged
context: dirty reflects staged writes
context: dirty is false with no staged writes
context: commit flushes staged writes to the base
ambient: a closure captures its provider's binding, resolved after the provider returns
text literal
bool literal
arithmetic precedence (2*3+4)
comparison (3 > 1)
integer division truncates (7/2)
integer division truncates toward zero (-7/2)
modulo keeps the dividend's sign (-7%3)
return of a nothing-valued call exits the function
text equality
access floor: property access on null fails closed (null.role == X is false)
access floor: null property access propagates (null.a.b == X is false, no throw)
logical and (false)
logical or (true)
object prop access
dynamic field access sys.field(obj, name)
sys.humanize a camelCase prop name
sys.new builds a fresh object from a TYPE descriptor — a scalar prop gets its baseType default (int → 0)
cases.text: sys.new({ props: [ { name: "n", baseType: "int" }, { name: "label", baseType: "text" }, { name: "ref", baseType: "object", target: "T" } ] }).n

cases.name: sys.new mints a COMPLETE object — a single-object (reference) prop is present and null (twin: DbBridge loads an unset reference as null)
cases.text: sys.field(sys.new({ props: [ { name: "ref", baseType: "object", target: "T" } ] }), "ref") == null

cases.name: sys.new mints a COMPLETE object — a SET prop is present and an empty (iterable) collection (twin: DbBridge loads an absent set as an empty set)
sys.new mints a COMPLETE object — a DICTIONARY prop is present and an empty collection (twin: DbBridge loads an absent dict as an empty dict)
sys.new on a scalar-DICTIONARY prop descriptor mints a single `value` defaulted by `element` (text → "")
sys.nest joins a base path and a segment, trimming a trailing slash
sys.nest appends a (text) segment to a non-root base
sys.nest appends an INT segment to a non-root base (stringifies it)
sys.nest joins a (text) dictionary key — the same primitive a dict route reuses
sys.segment index 0 of a rooted path is the empty leading segment
sys.segment index 1 of a rooted path
sys.segment index 2 of a rooted path (the route key)
sys.segment out of range is empty
sys.segment of the bare root, index 1, is empty
sys.segment of the empty path, index 0, is empty
sys.toInt parses a plain number
sys.toInt drops leading zeros ("007" -> 7)
sys.toInt of a non-numeric text is 0
sys.toInt of the empty text is 0
sys.toInt parses a leading minus sign ("-3" -> -3)
sys.toInt(sys.segment(...)) — the routing expression: read a route int from a path
sys.id reads an object's intrinsic id (a fresh object literal mints -1 identically on both twins)
object-field assignment (obj.x = 9) sets the field
immediately-invoked function (n => n + 1)(41)
where filters a list (preserving order)
where then orderBy chained over a list
where over the SAME collection with a predicate closing over an outer var disambiguates per value (memo-key closure)
cases.comment: pick(x) = nums.where(n => n == x) called for x=10 then RETURNING x=20. The lambda AST node — and the collection nums (one id, captured) — are identical across both calls, so keying the where memo on (collection id, lambda id) alone returns pick(10)'s [10] for pick(20). Folding the captured x into the key makes them distinct; the result must be [20], not [10].

cases.name: orderBy over the SAME collection with a selector closing over an outer var disambiguates per value (memo-key closure)
cases.comment: srt(sign) = nums.orderBy(n => n * sign) called for sign=1 (ascending) then RETURNING sign=-1 (descending). Same lambda node + same captured collection across both calls, so a (collection id, lambda id) key returns srt(1)'s ascending order for srt(-1). Folding the captured sign into the key disambiguates; the result must be the descending [3, 2, 1].

cases.name: single returns the one matching member
single returns null when no member matches
single on an empty collection returns null (pins the empty-receiver contract)
a tag-invoked component is recognized by name and binds its attribute to the param
cases.comment: <c d={42}> is a tag-invoked component: the name `c` resolves to a function, so it is invoked (not rendered as an HTML element), and the `d` attribute binds to the `d` param BY NAME — c records it in `seen` and the probe reads 42. Both twins recognize and bind identically. (Run-once-ACROSS-re-renders is a client-cache behavior — C#'s Memoize is write-only, the server renders once — so it is proven by the SelfHostedUi Gherkin scenario, not here.)

cases.name: a component defined inside another component is usable as a tag
cases.comment: outer is a component whose constructor defines a LOCAL function `inner` and whose view renders <inner>. Name-resolution finds the captured local, so <inner> is recognized as a component and its body runs (hits -> 1) — proving a component can be defined in a constructor and used in render. <inner> sits at slot comp:0/0, nested under outer's comp:0.

cases.name: two sibling components get distinct slot keys
cases.comment: The SAME component `c` appears at two sibling slots in one render (comp:0 with d=1, comp:1 with d=2), each recording its arg in `last`. Both twins must compute DISTINCT slot keys per static child index: if they collided, the client (which cache-hits) would return the first sibling's result for the second and leave last=1, while the write-only C# side would run both and reach last=2 — so a collision diverges the twins. With distinct keys both reach last=2. This is the one cross-twin-observable check of slot-key uniqueness (run-once itself is client-only — see case above).

cases.name: a component inside a foreach gets a distinct slot per row (by member identity)
cases.comment: The SAME component `c` is rendered once per row of a 2-object list, recording each member's `n`. The foreach folds each member's identity (its object id) into the slot path, so the two rows get DISTINCT slot keys and BOTH run — last reaches 20. If foreach rows shared a key, the client would cache-hit the first row for the second (last stuck at 10) and diverge from the write-only C# side (20). This is the lists/keys foundation that lets the generic UI's per-prop components (refEditor/setTable/dictTable, rendered in `foreach p in meta.props`) be tag-invoked.

cases.name: an explicit key on a component folds into its slot identity
cases.comment: <c key={...}> — `key` is a reserved directive (not a param): its value folds into the component's slot key, so the SAME component at the SAME render-tree position but with a DIFFERENT key is a DISTINCT identity. Rendered twice (key 1, then key 2): both setups run, bumps=2 on both twins. If `key` did NOT fold in, the two renders would share a slot and the client would cache-hit the first (bumps=1) and diverge from write-only C# (2). The opt-in RESET itself (same path, key changes -> fresh state) is a client behavior, proven by the SelfHostedUi scenario.

cases.name: a component in value position is recognized and returns its view value
cases.comment: A component tag in VALUE / return position (not a tag-child) is recognized by name-resolution and run slot-keyed, returning its view VALUE (not a spliced child list). Here `c` returns its bound arg as the view, so `<c d={5}>` evaluated as a value yields 5 — proving value-position recognition + by-name binding + value-return, identically on both twins. (On main, value position is NOT recognized, so `<c>` would be an HTML element / non-scalar tag — this case is new with slice 4b. It unblocks the generic UI's synthesized ROOT views being tag-invoked.)

cases.name: a seeded component renders the seeded state, not its setup default
cases.comment: Client data layer, slice 1a: a STATEFUL component `c` (its setup mints `var state = { x: 0 }` and returns a render closure that reads `state.x`) is rendered in value position — so it occupies slot `comp:` (empty slot path). The case SEEDS that slot's `state` var with `{ x: 7 }` (a whole-object overwrite). After the setup runs and BEFORE the view is invoked, both twins overwrite `state` in the setup's captured scope, so the view reads 7, NOT the default 0. This proves the server can REPRODUCE the client's exact component state (the seed-consumption half of render-as-planner) — identically on both interpreters. The SHIP of state from the client + the refetch threading are later (client-only) slices, not provable here.

cases.name: a function compared to null is not equal (fn != null)
cases.comment: A function value compared to null yields a bool by reference identity: a function is never null, so `fn != null` is true. Pins the C#/TS twin divergence where C# (ExecFunction.Value => this, ExecNull.Value => null, compared via object.Equals) was correct but the TS twin's getCompareValue had no `fn` case and threw. Both twins must now agree.

cases.name: a function compared to null is not == null (fn == null)
cases.comment: The mirror of the != case: `fn == null` is false (a function is never null). Same divergence guard — the TS getCompareValue `fn` case must return the function value so equality is by reference identity, matching C#'s object.Equals over ExecFunction.Value => this.

cases.name: atomic-commit Step B: set.add of a transient draft under a STAGING ctx stages into ctx.creates (in-memory; ctx.dirty true)
cases.comment: Under a STAGING ctx (ctx.new), adding a TRANSIENT (sys.new, id<0) draft to a set STAGES the create — it joins ctx.creates instead of firing/persisting — so ctx.dirty (which counts staged creates) is true. Strictly in-memory: the conformance harness has no store/WS, so 'not persisted' is inherent; the observable is that the create registered as staged work (dirty), identically on both twins. The C# AddToCollection staging branch is gated on the ctx ALONE (not the store), so it runs here exactly as the client twin's — the whole point of the lockstep branch. `box` is an object whose `items` is an empty set (sys.new of a set-prop descriptor); `draft` is a fresh transient object.

cases.name: atomic-commit Step B: committing a STAGING ctx clears its staged create (ctx.dirty false after commit)
cases.comment: After staging a create (set.add of a transient draft, as above) a ctx.commit() on the OUTERMOST staging ctx flushes + clears it — so ctx.dirty (staged creates count) is false again. The send is a server-side no-op (no wsHooks in the harness, and the C# final commit never persists — the teeth live in WsHandler.HandleCommit), so the only observable is that the staged create was consumed, identically on both twins. Same set/draft shape as the prior case.

cases.name: atomic-commit Step B: a NESTED ctx's commit flushes its create UP into the parent (the two-level unit-of-work)
cases.comment: The two-level core of the nested-unit-of-work model: a create staged in an INNER ctx.new() (nested under another staging ctx) transfers UP into the PARENT on the inner's commit, instead of persisting — so the OUTER ctx is then dirty (its creates hold the transferred one) while the inner is cleared. This is the `nested == true` commit branch (parent is a staging ctx, !live), the twin-divergence-prone spot the single-level staging cases don't reach. `outer`/`inner` are bound as vars so each `.dirty`/`.commit()` is addressable independently; `ambient ctx = inner` makes the inner the nearest staging ctx, so set.add stages into it. Render: add → inner.commit() (flush up) → outer.dirty == true on both twins.

cases.name: atomic-commit Step B: after a nested flush-up, the OUTER commit consumes the transferred create (dirty false)
cases.comment: The completion of the nested case: the inner commit flushed its create up into the OUTER (dirty true above); committing the OUTER then consumes it (the OUTER is the outermost — its parent is the live root — so it 'persists', a no-op in the harness) and clears its creates, so outer.dirty is false. Proves the transferred create lives in the parent until the OUTER commits, identically on both twins. Same two-level setup; render: add → inner.commit() (up) → outer.commit() (consume) → outer.dirty == false.

cases.name: unary ! negates true to false
unary ! negates false to true
unary ! binds tighter than == : !true == false is (!true) == false
double unary ! : !!false is false
string concatenation: two text operands
string concatenation: text + int stringifies the int
int addition preserved when neither operand is text
ternary: true condition selects the then branch
ternary: false condition selects the else branch
ternary: a comparison condition selecting a text branch
ternary short-circuits: the untaken branch is never evaluated (an undefined symbol in it does not throw)
ternary: right-associative — a nested ternary in the then branch
block lambda executes all statements (two property writes, then read back)
REGRESSION (component keying, surface 1): one render-prop invoked at two component slots runs twice
cases.comment: `wrap(body)` is a component whose view invokes its render-prop `body()` at TWO distinct child slots of its `<div>` (div child 0 and child 1). Each invocation renders <item label="X"> which APPENDS to the `seen` text. Both invocations are distinct render-tree slots, so the inner component must run TWICE and `seen` must reach "XX". On main it does NOT: a render-prop `body()` is invoked through the function-call path, whose memo key was (lambda fn-id, args) with NO slot segment — so the SECOND `body()` cache-hits the FIRST and returns its view without re-running <item>. The inner component runs once and `seen` stays "X". This diverges the twins: the client (read-back memo) cache-hits and yields "X"; the write-only C# side re-runs both and yields the CORRECT "XX". The fix folds the live render slotPath into the `fn:` call memo key (codeExec executeCall / CodeExecutor CallFunction, byte-identically across the twins): the two child slots get distinct slotPaths, so the two invocations get distinct keys and each re-runs.

cases.name: REGRESSION (component keying, surface 2): each foreach row's fresh render-prop runs its own component
cases.comment: Two foreach rows each invoke `menu(body)`, passing a FRESH per-row render-prop closure that renders <item label={row.n}> (the KebabMenu(body) shape). Each row appends its n to the `seen` text, so both rows running yields "12". On main only row 1's <item> runs: the per-row closures are SEPARATE objects but share one lambda AST id, and `body()` was invoked through the function-call path whose memo key was (lambda fn-id, args) — identical for every row, with NO foreach-row/slot segment. So row 2's `body()` cache-hits row 1's and renders row 1's <item> again; row 2's own <item> never runs and `seen` stays "1". The slot path DOES carry the row identity (comp:0/row<id>/...), but the render-prop invocation ignored it. This diverges the twins (client cache-hits -> "1"; write-only C# re-runs each -> CORRECT "12"). The fix folds the live render slotPath into the `fn:` call memo key (byte-identically across the twins): each row's invocation carries its `row<id>` slot segment, so the per-row `body()` calls get distinct keys and each row's <item> runs.

cases.name: REGRESSION (component keying, surface 3): each foreach row's SCALAR render-prop runs once per row
cases.comment: Like surface 2 but the per-row render-prop `body` returns its `row.n` as BARE TEXT (no <item> component, no tag built inside the body's bracket) — the scalar render-prop shape a result/side-effect heuristic CANNOT detect (the body's `fn:` compute produces no display of its own, so a 'did this render?' signal stays false and the entry still cross-collides). `menu`'s view APPENDS `body()` to `seen`, so two rows running yields "12". On main only row 1's `body()` runs: every row's fresh `body` closure shares one lambda AST id and is invoked with the same (empty) args, so id+args alone collide with NO row/slot segment — row 2's `body()` cache-hits row 1's and re-appends row 1's "1"; `seen` stays "1" (TS read-cache) while the write-only C# re-runs each and yields "12". The slotPath fold fixes it: each row carries its `row<id>` slot segment in the `fn:` key, so the per-row scalar `body()` calls are distinct and each appends its own n.

cases.name: sys.renderTree — nested elements, ordered attrs (literal applied / non-literal + event skipped), out-of-range int + reserved data-node attrs skipped, literal + chip + empty leaves, data-node stamping
cases.comment: The CLIENT-COMPUTABLE canvas (M12). renderTree walks a MetaNode row (tag/expr/order + attrs/children sets) into a tag tree, both twins from the SAME row data — so this pins the literal rules, the chip/empty leaf shapes, the event/non-literal attr skips, child ordering by `order`, and data-node provenance on EVERY element. The root <div> has: class="box" (string literal, applied), id=a+b (non-literal, skipped), onClick="go" (event attr, skipped though literal), big=9999999999 (an int-SHAPED source outside Int32 range — review fix 1: non-literal, skipped, no overflow crash on either twin), and data-node="clobber" (a user attr named the RESERVED provenance attr — review fix 3: skipped so it can never clobber the id renderTree itself stamps); its children are given OUT of order and sorted by `order` to <span>, chip, empty. The <span> has tabindex=2 (int literal, applied) and one string-literal leaf ("hi" -> text). The chip leaf's expr is non-literal (a+b -> span.expr-chip with the raw source); the last child has neither tag nor expr (span.expr-chip.is-empty '(empty)'). data-node ids are the inline literals' transient negative ids, assigned identically by both twins (props-then-object, key-then-value for arrays), so the serialized trees match. Attributes are serialized ordinally-sorted (DOM attr order is a browser concern, verified in the browser scenario, not here).

expect.value: hia + b(empty)

cases.name: sys.renderTree — a `for` row (kind="for") renders its NO-CTX TEMPLATE (M12 S6a)
cases.comment: S6a control-flow rows: a MetaNode with kind="for" renders as a for-template wrapper (data-node=<row id>) holding a badge (the item var name + the collection SOURCE as an unevaluated expr-chip carrying the SAME row id) and the body rendered ONCE. The body's leaf `note.title` is a non-literal bare symbol (the item var is unbound in this template mode — S6b's row-scope eval is a separate slice) -> its own expr-chip with the LEAF's own id. Ids: the leaf's ExecuteObject runs first (props-then-object), then the children array, then the for-row's own object id last — LastId starts at 0 in a fresh case, so leaf=-2, for-row=-4 on BOTH twins.

expect.value:
notedb.notes
note.title

cases.name: sys.renderTree — an `if` row (kind="if") with an else renders BOTH branches, then/else labeled (M12 S6a)
cases.comment: kind="if" renders as an if-template wrapper (data-node=<row id>) with the condition SOURCE as an unevaluated expr-chip (carrying the SAME row id — never guesses the taken branch, S6b's concern) and BOTH branches wrapped + labeled then/else. Both leaves are STRING LITERALS (\"Done\"/\"Pending\") so they render as plain text, not chips (today's exact literal-leaf rule, unchanged). Ids: children's leaf=-2, its array=-3, elseChildren's leaf=-5, its array=-6, the if-row's own id=-7 (props-then-object, key-then-value for arrays) on BOTH twins.

expect.value:
note.done
thenDone
elsePending

cases.name: sys.renderTree — an `if` row with an EMPTY elseChildren omits the else branch entirely (M12 S6a)
cases.comment: elseChildren present but with zero members (the shape a fresh `if` row starts in — no else authored yet) renders NO else-branch element at all, not an empty one — the operator sees only a `then` branch until '+ else' is used. Ids: children's leaf=-2, its array=-3, the empty elseChildren array=-4 (no items to consume ids), the if-row's own id=-5.

expect.value:
true
thenYes

cases.name: sys.renderTree — a `for` row containing an `if` row (NESTED control-flow templates) pins twin id-assignment through the nesting (M12 S6a review fix 3)
cases.comment: A for-row's body is itself an if-row (condition note.done, one leaf then-branch note.title, no else) — proving the recursive BuildRenderTree/renderTreeNode dispatch (kind check re-fires on a child that is itself a control-flow row, not just a plain element/leaf) and that id-assignment order stays twin-identical through the extra nesting level. Ids (props-then-object, key-then-value for arrays, LastId starts at 0): the innermost leaf's ExecuteObject runs first -> leaf=-3; then the if-row's `children` array=-4; the if-row's `elseChildren` (empty)=-5; the if-row's own object id=-6; then the for-row's `children` array (holding the already-built if-row)=-7; the for-row's own object id=-8 last.

expect.value:
notedb.notes
note.done
thennote.title

cases.name: sys.renderTree with an eval context — db-rooted exprs evaluate (single/where+any/orderBy+single), an attr expr applies, a store-backed builtin chips (tier 2), an expr absent from the map chips (tier 3)
cases.comment: The canvas EVALUATION pivot (M12 CANVAS-EVAL-1). renderTree(node, ctx) consumes a { db, exprs } eval context: each non-literal leaf/attr source is looked up in ctx.exprs (content-addressed by text) -> its serialized AST JSON is deserialized and run through ExecuteValue over an ISOLATED scope binding db=ctx.db on a store-less executor, so both twins agree. Tier 1 (AST + clean eval): db.title -> attr id="Hi"; db.settings.single(s=>true).title -> "Hello"; db.notes.where(n=>n.done).any(n=>n.rank==0) -> "true"; db.notes.orderBy(n=>n.rank).single(n=>n.done).title -> "C" (collections over the synthetic graph, values in hand — the verified caveat). Tier 2 (AST + throw): sys.extent("X") is in the map but store-backed -> throws on the store-less executor -> its raw-source chip. Tier 3 (no AST): db.notes is NOT in the map (an edited-but-unrefreshed expr) -> its chip. data-node ids are the inline node literals negative ids, assigned identically by both twins. Proves the no-second-engine pivot: the walk evaluates NOTHING itself, delegating every eval to ExecuteValue.

cases.name: && short-circuits — the right operand is NOT evaluated when the left is false
cases.comment: The universal null-guard idiom `x != null && f(x)` depends on this: the right side here reads an UNBOUND symbol, which throws on both twins if evaluated. Pre-fix (eager operands) this case throws; with short-circuit it is simply false. Found via a real-data break: an Instance with no design made every design page throw 'id() expects an object' straight through its `i.design != null &&` guard.

cases.name: || short-circuits — the right operand is NOT evaluated when the left is true
cases.comment: The dual of the && case: an unbound-symbol right side never evaluates when the left already decides true.

cases.name: the null-guard idiom — `x != null && f(x)` never evaluates f(null)
cases.comment: The app-shaped pin for the real-data break: sys.id(null) throws if evaluated, so this expression only yields false under short-circuit. This is the guard every app writes (the designer's own `i.design != null && sys.id(i.design) == ...`, which threw 'id() expects an object' over an instance with no design).

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx — a `for` row EVALUATES its collection and instantiates the body PER ITEM with the loop var bound (M12 S6b)
cases.comment: The row-scope evaluation pivot. A parent <ul> wraps a for-row (the shape a real render has — a control-flow row is a CHILD of an element). renderTree(ul, ctx): the for-row's `collection` (db.notes) evaluates against the seed graph -> a 2-item collection; the body (an <li> holding the leaf `note.title`) is rendered ONCE PER ITEM with {note -> the item} layered onto {db} in the isolated eval scope, so note.title resolves to each item's title. The instances REPLACE the template (no badge, no for-template wrapper) — the for-row returns an ARRAY value spliced FLAT into the <ul> children (exactly how the real foreach splices rows; SerializeChild/ui.ts flatten an array child). PROVENANCE: both <li> instances carry the SAME data-node (the body <li>'s own MetaNode id, -8) — N instances : 1 template row (S6a). The walk mints NOTHING into LastId, so the output is deterministic regardless of item count; the data-node ids come only from the input node literals (ul=-12, li=-8). Twin-identical.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx — NESTED `for` rows STACK bindings; the inner collection references the OUTER loop var (M12 S6b)
cases.comment: Stacked row scope (wrapped in a parent <ul>): an outer `foreach cat in db.cats` whose body is an inner `foreach item in cat.items` (the inner collection reads the OUTER loop var `cat`). For each outer cat, {cat -> item} is layered onto {db}, and the inner `for`'s collection `cat.items` evaluates against that binding; then {item -> ...} STACKS on top for the inner body leaf `item.label`. Seed: cats=[{items:[{label:"a1"}]},{items:[{label:"b1"},{label:"b2"}]}] -> the flat instance stream is a1,b1,b2 (each in its own <span>). Proves nested bindings accumulate (the outer item is visible to the inner collection AND the inner body) and that an array-of-arrays child flattens recursively. All three <span>s across both outer iterations share the span's ONE MetaNode data-node id (-9); the <ul> is -15.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx — an `if` row evaluates its condition and renders ONLY the taken branch, flat + unlabeled (then / else) (M12 S6b)
cases.comment: A parent <div> wraps an if-row. renderTree(div, ctx) with condition db.flag=true renders ONLY the `then` branch (the string-literal leaf "On") FLAT into the <div> — no if-template wrapper, no then/else labels, no condition chip (real content). The else branch ("Off") is NOT rendered. Twin of the false case below (same tree, flag=false -> "Off"). data-node: div=-11.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx — an `if` row with a FALSE condition renders ONLY the else branch (M12 S6b)
cases.comment: Same shape as the then case (wrapped in a <div>) but db.flag=false -> ONLY the else-branch leaf "Off" renders, flat. Pins that a false condition selects elseChildren (and an empty elseChildren would render nothing). data-node: div=-11.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx — an `if` whose condition evaluates to a NON-BOOL DEGRADES to the S6a both-branches template (M12 S6b)
cases.comment: The truthiness guard: db.title evaluates to a TEXT, not a bool. deenv truthiness belongs to the interpreter, not the canvas, so the canvas must NOT invent a taken branch — it DEGRADES to the S6a both-branches labeled template (condition chip + then/else). (An UNEVALUABLE condition — a source absent from the exprs map, or one that throws — degrades identically, since evalCtxExpr returns null.) Ids identical to the S6a if-with-else template case: children leaf=-2, its array=-3, elseChildren leaf=-5, its array=-6, if-row=-7.

expect.value:
db.title
thenOn
elseOff

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx — a `for` whose collection is UNEVALUABLE degrades to the S6a template (M12 S6b)
cases.comment: The collection source `db.missing` is NOT in the exprs map (an edited-but-unrefreshed / non-navigable source) -> evalCtxExpr returns null -> the for row DEGRADES to the S6a NO-CTX template (for-template wrapper + badge with the item var + the collection chip; the body leaf note.title chips too, item unbound). NEVER guesses. Ids: leaf=-2, for-row's children array=-3, for-row=-4 (as the S6a for-template case).

expect.value:
notedb.missing
note.title

cases.name: sys.renderTree with fns — a bound literal param evaluates, a missing param defaults to null, and an attr present but UNEVALUABLE leaves the param UNBOUND so the body chips (M12 F2)
cases.comment: The F2 param-binding pivot. `fns` carries one MetaFn Badge(label) whose single-root body is the bare leaf `label` (so the expansion's rendered content IS whatever `label` binds to — no ternary needed to observe all three outcomes). A parent <div> invokes <Badge/> three times: (1) label="hi" (a STRING LITERAL attr) binds via LiteralValue even though no source for it rides ctx.exprs — renders the real text "hi"; (2) no `label` attr at all — BindParams-style, binds ExecNull (runtime truth) — a null leaf renders as EMPTY text (ChildText's non-scalar fallback), i.e. nothing between its neighbors; (3) label={counter} — a NON-literal attr whose source "counter" is deliberately ABSENT from ctx.exprs (an edited-but-unrefreshed source, tier-3) — evalCtxExpr misses, so the param is left OUT of the body bindings entirely, and the body's `label` reference then MISSES SCOPE in the isolated eval and throws → chip, holding the raw source "label" (the body leaf's own text) — the one deliberate divergence from runtime (never guess a value). Every <Badge/> invocation drops its own children (none authored) and expands the SAME body leaf row (one MetaNode, shared id), so all three chip/text outcomes share no data-node collision with each other. `key`/extra-attr handling is unexercised here (covered by BindParams' own tests) — this case is scoped to the three param-binding outcomes.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with fns — a walk-local binding (a for row's loop var) SHADOWS a same-named fns row, rendering a literal element instead of expanding (M12 F2 grill E1)
cases.comment: Runtime resolution stops at the first scope binding, and a non-function binding makes the tag a plain element (TryResolveComponent) — the canvas must be equally honest. `fns` carries a MetaFn literally named "note" (its body — a <span> holding the literal "fn-body" — would be an unmistakable tell if it wrongly expanded). The render tree is a parent <div> wrapping a `for` row (`item`="note", `collection`="db.notes", one seeded item) whose body is a bare <note/> element (no attrs, no children). Because the loop var is named "note" — THE SAME NAME as the fns row — buildFor's itemBindings carries "note" as a walk-local binding, which SHADOWS the fns lookup entirely: <note/> renders as a literal empty element (data-node stamped, no children, certainly no "fn-body" text), never resolving against `fns` at all.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with fns — two fns rows sharing a name tie-break to the LAST one in `order` (M12 F2, mirrors DefineFunction's last-wins)
cases.comment: `fns` carries TWO MetaFn rows both named "Dup" — order=0's body is the literal leaf "first", order=1's body is the literal leaf "second". A <div> invokes <Dup/> once. ResolveFn picks the LAST one in `order` (mirrors DefineFunction's silent last-wins for the mid-edit window before projection's duplicate refusal fires) — so the canvas shows "second", never "first".

cases.name: sys.renderTree with fns — a self-invoking component DEGRADES to a component chip past the depth cap (M12 F2 grill E4)
cases.comment: `fns` carries ONE MetaFn Rec() whose own single-root body IS an invocation of itself (<Rec/>, no attrs/children) — genuine unbounded tag-name recursion with no data to bound it (no ctx is even needed; the recursion is purely structural). A parent <div> invokes <Rec/> once. The expansion depth cap (32) bounds it: the walk expands 32 levels deep (each level re-resolving the SAME shared body row against `fns`), and the 33rd resolution attempt — still the SAME body row, since a fn's body is one static MetaNode re-walked every level — hits `expansion.Depth >= 32` and renders an honest `span.component-chip` (name badge "Rec") instead of recursing further (or hanging, or throwing a stack overflow on either twin). Every intermediate level contributes nothing to the serialized text (each is an inert array wrapper around the next), so the <div>'s entire content is exactly the one chip.

cases.name: call-depth guard: unbounded named self-recursion throws a catchable interpreter error, not a stack overflow (M12 FG)
cases.comment: A named fn literal self-binds its own name into the enclosing scope BEFORE its body runs (ExecuteFunction/executeFunction: `if (function.Name != null) scope.Items[Name] = fn`), so `Rec` calling `Rec()` in its own body is genuine unbounded interpreter-level recursion — no data bounds it, unlike the F2 canvas-expansion case above (which is a SEPARATE mechanism, ExpansionState.Depth, capped at 32). Pre-FG this recurses forever: an uncatchable StackOverflowException on the C# twin (process death) and an eventually-thrown, twin-DIVERGENT RangeError on the TS twin. FG's call-depth guard (RunBody, both twins) makes it a normal, twin-IDENTICAL CodeRuntimeException/Error at the shared threshold (256) — the grill F3c finding this slice exists to fix.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns — call-position evaluation: a leaf calls a design fn, the real interpreter computes it (M12 F3)
cases.comment: ctx.fns binds "double" (fn double(n) { return n * 2 }) as a callable into the isolated eval scope (EvaluateCtxExpr/evalCtxExpr), exactly like a named `fn` statement binds itself. The leaf's non-literal expr "double(3)" is in ctx.exprs (a CodeCall AST) and evaluates through the REAL interpreter's ExecuteValue/CallFunction (not a second engine) to 6.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns — a recursive helper over finite data evaluates correctly (M12 F3)
cases.comment: sumTo(n) = n<=0 ? 0 : n + sumTo(n-1), bound into the SAME evalScope it closes over (ExecFunction.Scope = evalScope), so its self-call resolves at call time through the shared scope — mutual/self recursion works. sumTo(4) = 4+3+2+1+0 = 10, well under the FG call-depth guard (256).

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns — a RUNAWAY recursive helper past the FG call-depth guard CHIPS, not a crash (M12 F3 / F3c)
cases.comment: loop(n) unconditionally calls loop(n+1) — genuine unbounded interpreter recursion, no data bounds it. FG's call-depth guard (RunBody/runBody, threshold 256) throws a NORMAL catchable CodeRuntimeException/Error at the limit; EvaluateCtxExpr/evalCtxExpr's try/catch converts it to null, and the leaf falls to its ordinary chip (raw source "loop(0)") — never a stack overflow, never silence.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns — a call to an fn NOT in ctx.fns chips (M12 F3)
cases.comment: "mystery" is not bound anywhere in the isolated eval scope (ctx.fns is empty, no db, no ambient) — the symbol lookup misses scope and throws "Variable not found", caught by EvaluateCtxExpr/evalCtxExpr and turned into the leaf's ordinary chip (raw source "mystery(1)").

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns — a component called in expression position (plain call syntax, legal at runtime) SPLICES its tag as content (M12 F3)
cases.comment:
NoteCard(title) returns
  • {title}
    • — an ordinary CodeTag executed by the REAL interpreter (TryResolveComponent has nothing named "li" in scope, so it's a plain element). NoteCard("Hi") evaluates to an ExecTag, not a scalar — BuildRenderTree/renderTreeNode splice it as CONTENT (an ExecArray riding the leaf row's own id, the F2 idiom) instead of ChildText's usual (empty) non-scalar fallback, so the canvas shows the real
  • Hi
    • , not a chip and not nothing.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns AND live fns — MATCHING fingerprints show NO staleness banner (M12 F3b)
cases.comment: The live `fns` 3rd-arg row (name=Foo, params="", body="fn-body") has the IDENTICAL raw fields the ctx-shipped fingerprint was computed from, so FnsStale/fnsStale's name+content comparison matches and renderTree returns its plain result — no banner spliced ahead of it.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns AND live fns — a MUTATED fn row's fingerprint MISMATCHES, showing the staleness banner (M12 F3b)
cases.comment: Same as the matching case above, except the live row's leaf text changed ("fn-body" -> "fn-body-EDITED") — the SAME edit that changes NO call-site text (so a call-position eval would silently keep using the STALE ctx-shipped body). FnsStale/fnsStale recomputes the fingerprint from the LIVE row (dep-recorded) and it no longer matches the shipped "fp", so renderTree splices ONE span.stale-fns-banner ahead of the tree.

expect.value:
Components changed — call results may be stale. Refresh values.
Hello

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns — a row binding (a `for` loop var) SHADOWS a same-named fn in call position (M12 F3, mirrors runtime scoping)
cases.comment: `fns` carries a fn literally named "note" (returning the tell-tale text "fn-called"); a `for item="note" collection="db.items"` row (one seeded int item) binds the loop var "note" -> 1 into the row scope. EvaluateCtxExpr/evalCtxExpr binds fns BEFORE bindings, so bindings WIN (mirrors runtime: a scope item shadows an outer fn of the same name) — note(1) then calls the INT 1, which throws "Target of a call is not a function", caught — the leaf chips its raw source "note(1)", never evaluating to "fn-called".

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns AND live fns — an UNNAMED fn row (F1's "+ Component" mid-authoring mint) is EXCLUDED from the staleness comparison, so it never blocks a match (M12 F3b review fix)
cases.comment: The live `fns` 3rd arg carries TWO rows: "Foo" (matching the shipped fingerprint, same as the plain matching case) AND an unnamed row (name="") with a DELIBERATELY DIFFERENT body — a false pass would only occur if the unnamed row were correctly EXCLUDED, not if it were accidentally content-matched. FnsStale/fnsStale skip an empty name entirely (an unnamed fn has no call sites, so it cannot make any call result stale, and ctx.fns can never carry an unnamed entry either — a design with an unnamed fn also fails projection outright) — so the comparison is exactly {Foo: fp} vs {Foo: fp}, a match, and renderTree returns its plain result with NO banner.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with ctx.fns AND live fns — a MUTATED state var's init MISMATCHES the fingerprint, showing the staleness banner (M12 V1)
cases.comment: Same fn "Foo" as the plain matching/mismatch F3b cases above, but this time the BODY text is unchanged and only a state VAR's init differs (shipped fp computed with vars=[{v, init:"1"}]; the LIVE row's var v now has init "2") — proving the fingerprint (SchemaBridge.FnFingerprints / CodeExecutor.FnFingerprint / codeExec.ts fnFingerprint) folds `vars` in, not just params/body: a vars-only edit is enough to trip FnsStale/fnsStale on its own.

expect.value:
Components changed — call results may be stale. Refresh values.
Hello

cases.name: sys.renderTree with fns — a STATEFUL MetaFn (carrying `vars`) expands with its param bound AND its state var bound to its INIT value (M12 V1b, flips the V1 pin)
cases.comment: FLIPS the V1 pin above: `fns` carries ONE stateful MetaFn Counter(label) whose `vars` holds a state var "count" (init "0"). A parent <div> invokes <Counter label="hi"/> once; the body wraps two leaves: `label` (the bound PARAM — a literal attr, evaluated via ctx.exprs against the bodyBindings — renders the real text "hi") and `count` (the STATE VAR — V1b's BindVars/bindVars now binds it AFTER the params, evaluating its init "0" via ctx.exprs under the accumulated bodyBindings, so the leaf resolves it like any other bound name and renders the real value "0" — no chip). A static canvas can only ever show INITIAL state, so this — a fresh live instance's mount value — IS the truth; ctx.exprs carries the two NEW sources this slice needs: "0" (the var's init, an int literal — BindVars has no literal shortcut, so even a literal init needs an AST here, matching RenderExprSources' "collect broadly" stance) and "count" (the leaf's own reference, a plain symbol read).

cases.name: sys.renderTree with a design — a DESIGN-LEVEL var referenced in a render leaf evaluates to its INIT value (M12 V1b)
cases.comment: design.vars carries ONE top-level ui var x (init "5"), no fns at all (tolerated absent — the V1 OrderedMembersOptional precedent, now exercised for `fns` too via ReadNodePropOptional/readNodePropOptional). BindVars/bindVars binds it at the walk ROOT before the render tree walk starts, so the leaf `x` resolves against the seeded root bindings and renders the real value "5" — the design-var half of V1b's premise (a static canvas can only ever show INITIAL state).

cases.name: sys.renderTree with a design — a design var's init referencing an EARLIER design var evaluates SEQUENTIALLY (M12 V1b)
cases.comment: design.vars carries a (init "2", order 0) then b (init "a + 1", order 1). BindVars/bindVars processes vars in `order`, binding a=2 BEFORE evaluating b's init — so "a + 1" evaluates under {db, a: 2} and binds b=3. The leaf `b` renders "3", proving the sequential-binding obligation (an out-of-order pass would leave b's init unable to see a at all).

cases.name: sys.renderTree with fns — a component's OWN state var whose name COLLIDES with a param DEGRADES the whole expansion to a component chip (M12 V1b review fix, canvas-never-lies)
cases.comment: MetaFn Foo(x) declares a state var also named "x" (init "9") — the designer's own fnVarNameHint would warn "shadows a parameter" (advisory, not a projection refusal), so this authors cleanly and is genuinely reachable. <Foo x="1"/> binds the param x=1 FIRST (a literal attr); ExpandFn/expandFn's BindVars/bindVars call then finds "x" already bound (the runtime's OWN "already exists" condition — ExecuteVarDec would throw this at a real component's first mount) and REMOVES it rather than overwriting, returning collided=true. The caller never even walks the body: the WHOLE expansion degrades to the SAME component-chip an unnamed/bodyless/depth-capped fn already uses (never guess which binding — or the crash — the runtime would produce).

cases.name: sys.renderTree with a design — a BARE `var x` (empty init) binds ExecNull, rendering EMPTY (not a chip) (M12 V1b)
cases.comment: design.vars carries x with an EMPTY init ("") — the legal bare `var x` grammar. BindVars/bindVars binds it directly to ExecNull with NO evaluation at all (matching the runtime's ExecuteVarDec: a null CodeVarDec.Value defaults to ExecNull) — a legitimate null, not an unevaluable miss. The leaf `x` between two literal siblings renders as EMPTY text (ChildText's non-scalar fallback), so the two literals sit directly adjacent with nothing between them and NO chip — proving a bare var is bound, not unbound.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with a design — an UNEVALUABLE design-var init leaves that var UNBOUND (its references chip); a SIBLING var is unaffected (M12 V1b)
cases.comment: design.vars carries a (init "broken", order 0) and b (init "5", order 1). "broken" is DELIBERATELY ABSENT from ctx.exprs (an edited-but-unrefreshed source, tier-3) — BindVars/bindVars' own EvaluateCtxExpr/evalCtxExpr call misses the map and returns null, so `a` is left OUT of the root bindings entirely (never guess a value). Processing continues to b regardless (BindVars never aborts the loop on one var's miss) — b's init "5" evaluates cleanly and binds. The leaf `a` then misses scope in its own isolated eval and CHIPS (holding its raw source "a"); the leaf `b` renders the real value "5" — proving one var's failure never poisons another's binding.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with a design — a `for` loop var SHADOWS a same-named DESIGN var inside its body (M12 V1b, mirrors runtime scoping)
cases.comment: design.vars carries a top-level ui var literally named "note" (init "design-value", bound at the walk root). The render tree is a `for` row (item="note", collection="db.items", one seeded int item 1) whose body leaf reads `note`. BuildFor/buildFor's itemBindings START as a COPY of the root bindings (so "note" -> "design-value" rides in) then OVERWRITE itemBindings["note"] = 1 (the loop item) — the same walk-local-binding-shadows-outer-scope rule F3's row-binding-shadows-a-fn case already pins, now proven against a design var instead of a fn. The leaf renders "1" (the loop value), never "design-value", proving the shadow.

cases.name: sys.renderTree with a design — TWO design-level vars sharing a name COLLIDE at the walk root, leaving that name UNBOUND (M12 V1b review fix)
cases.comment: design.vars carries TWO rows both named "a" (init "2" order 0, init "3" order 1) — the live mid-edit window before ProjectRenderUi's own duplicate-name refusal would fire on save/publish (a real design can transiently reach this shape while the operator is typing). BindVars/bindVars processes order 0 first (binds a=2), then order 1 finds "a" ALREADY bound (the runtime's own "already exists" condition) and REMOVES it rather than overwriting — never guessing which of the two inits wins. The leaf `a` then misses scope in its own isolated eval and CHIPS (holding its raw source "a") — proving BOTH colliding definitions end up honestly unbound, not last-wins.

cases.name: sys.hasParam(params, name) — a comma-separated params list matches a TRIMMED token exactly (M12 V1 fnVarNameHint)
cases.comment: hasParam("note, extra", "extra") — the second token has a leading space in the raw text (comma-separated, MetaFn.params' own join format); trimming makes it match "extra" exactly.

cases.name: sys.hasParam(params, name) — no matching token returns false (M12 V1 fnVarNameHint)
